title: The Rover's Adieu
content:
A weary lot is thine, fair maid,
    A weary lot is thine!
    To pull the thorn thy brow to braid,
    And press the rue for wine.
    A lightsome eye, a soldier's mien,
    A feather of the blue,
    A doublet of the Lincoln green
    No more of me ye knew,
    My Love!
    No more of me ye knew.

    'This morn is merry June, I trow,
    The rose is budding fain;
    But she shall bloom in winter snow
    Ere we two meet again.'
    He turn'd his charger as he spake
    Upon the river shore,
    He gave the bridle-reins a shake,
    Said 'Adieu for evermore,
    My Love!
    And adieu for evermore.'
meaning:
This evocative piece by Walter Scott (Sir), titled "The Rover's Adieu", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
